ucs.title: [LLM] Storm-1811 email-bomb + Quick Assist/AnyDesk help-desk impersonation chain
ucs.description: Hunts Storm-1811's signature initial-access pattern: a sudden email-bombing flood of benign newsletter/subscription spam against a single mailbox, immediately followed by the user launching Quick Assist, AnyDesk, or TeamViewer after a Teams/voice 'help desk' callback — the precursor to Black Basta deployment.
ucs.splunk: | tstats `summariesonly` count from datamodel=Email.All_Email where All_Email.dest!="" by All_Email.dest, _time span=10m | rename All_Email.dest as recipient | where count>=50 | join type=inner recipient [| tstats `summariesonly` min(_time) as proc_time values(Processes.process_name) as proc_name values(Processes.user) as user from datamodel=Endpoint.Processes where Processes.process_name IN ("quickassist.exe","AnyDesk.exe","TeamViewer.exe","ScreenConnect.ClientService.exe","ScreenConnect.WindowsClient.exe") by Processes.user | rename Processes.user as recipient] | eval delta=proc_time-_time | where delta>=0 AND delta<=7200 | table _time, recipient, count, proc_name, proc_time, delta
ucs.kql:
let bombWindow = 10m;
let pivotWindow = 2h;
let bombed = EmailEvents
| where Timestamp > ago(7d)
| where DeliveryAction == "Delivered"
| summarize MailCount = count(), BombStart = min(Timestamp), BombEnd = max(Timestamp) by RecipientEmailAddress, bin(Timestamp, bombWindow)
| where MailCount >= 50;
let rmm = DeviceProcessEvents
| where Timestamp > ago(7d)
| where FileName in~ ("quickassist.exe","AnyDesk.exe","TeamViewer.exe","ScreenConnect.ClientService.exe","ScreenConnect.WindowsClient.exe","msra.exe")
| extend UserUPN = tolower(strcat(AccountName, "@", AccountDomain))
| project RmmTime = Timestamp, DeviceId, DeviceName, FileName, ProcessCommandLine, AccountName, UserUPN, InitiatingProcessFileName;
bombed
| extend RecipientEmailAddress = tolower(RecipientEmailAddress)
| join kind=inner (rmm) on $left.RecipientEmailAddress == $right.UserUPN
| where RmmTime between (BombStart .. (BombEnd + pivotWindow))
| project BombStart, BombEnd, MailCount, RecipientEmailAddress, RmmTime, DeviceName, FileName, ProcessCommandLine, InitiatingProcessFileName
| order by RmmTime asc
ucs.rationale: This is the literal Microsoft/Rapid7/Red Canary-documented Storm-1811 (Black Basta affiliate) intrusion playbook: email-bombing to drive a panicked 'IT' callback, then social-engineering Quick Assist or a similar RMM to gain hands-on-keyboard access. The two-stage join (mail flood + RMM launch by the same user within 2h) makes it nearly unique to Storm-1811 and adjacent help-desk-impersonation cre

ucs.title: [LLM] Storm-1811 Quick Assist / msra.exe spawning LOLBin downloaders & Black Basta loaders
ucs.description: Detects post-pretext hands-on-keyboard activity: once the Storm-1811 operator is connected via Quick Assist (quickassist.exe / msra.exe), they characteristically drop and execute curl.exe, PowerShell, or BITS to pull AntiSpam.zip, OneDriveStandaloneUpdater.exe, or signed-but-malicious DLLs (Black Basta / SystemBC / Cobalt Strike) — followed by rundll32 side-loading.
ucs.splunk: | tstats `summariesonly` count min(_time) as firstTime max(_time) as lastTime values(Processes.process) as cmdline values(Processes.parent_process_name) as parent values(Processes.user) as user from datamodel=Endpoint.Processes where Processes.parent_process_name IN ("quickassist.exe","msra.exe","AnyDesk.exe","ScreenConnect.ClientService.exe") AND (Processes.process_name IN ("powershell.exe","pwsh.exe","cmd.exe","curl.exe","certutil.exe","bitsadmin.exe","rundll32.exe","regsvr32.exe") OR Processes.process IN ("*Invoke-WebRequest*","*DownloadString*","*-urlcache*","*AntiSpam*","*OneDriveStandaloneUpdater*")) by Processes.dest Processes.user Processes.process_name Processes.parent_process_name | `drop_dm_object_name(Processes)` | convert ctime(firstTime) ctime(lastTime)
ucs.kql:
DeviceProcessEvents
| where Timestamp > ago(7d)
| where InitiatingProcessFileName in~ ("quickassist.exe","msra.exe","AnyDesk.exe","ScreenConnect.ClientService.exe","ScreenConnect.WindowsClient.exe","TeamViewer.exe")
| where FileName in~ ("powershell.exe","pwsh.exe","cmd.exe","curl.exe","certutil.exe","bitsadmin.exe","rundll32.exe","regsvr32.exe","mshta.exe")
  or ProcessCommandLine has_any ("Invoke-WebRequest","DownloadString","DownloadFile","-urlcache","-split","AntiSpam.zip","OneDriveStandaloneUpdater","netsupport","SystemBC")
| extend SuspiciousDownload = ProcessCommandLine has_any ("http://","https://",".zip",".dll",".exe")
| project Timestamp, DeviceName, AccountName, InitiatingProcessFileName, FileName, ProcessCommandLine, SuspiciousDownload, FolderPath
| join kind=leftouter (
    DeviceNetworkEvents
    | where Timestamp > ago(7d)
    | where InitiatingProcessFileName in~ ("powershell.exe","curl.exe","certutil.exe","bitsadmin.exe","rundll32.exe")
    | project NetTime = Timestamp, DeviceName, RemoteUrl, RemoteIP, InitiatingProcessFileName
) on DeviceName
| where isnull(NetTime) or (NetTime between (Timestamp .. Timestamp + 5m))
| order by Timestamp desc
ucs.rationale: Storm-1811 / Black Basta affiliate intrusions consistently show quickassist.exe or msra.exe as the parent of the second-stage downloader — a sequence that is essentially never benign (legitimate IT help-desk sessions don't curl payloads from external infra). Microsoft's 2024 Storm-1811 advisory and Red Canary's reporting both call out this exact parent→child chain (Quick Assist → curl/PowerShell →
